step_by_step_thinking:
Given the clear pattern where only affected females consistently pass the trait to their children, and considering that affected males tend not to reproduce or transmit through them, the evidence strongly supports X-linked recessive inheritance. There isn't sufficient data suggesting mitochondrial inheritance, nor does the pattern fit autosomal recessive or dominant modes well enough to warrant those conclusions. Hence, my agreement remains firm with option C being the best match.

Therefore, my answer is still Option C - X linked recessive. **(500 characters including spaces)**

raw_response:
Given the clear pattern where only affected females consistently pass the trait to their children, and considering that affected males tend not to reproduce or transmit through them, the evidence strongly supports X-linked recessive inheritance. There isn't sufficient data suggesting mitochondrial inheritance, nor does the pattern fit autosomal recessive or dominant modes well enough to warrant those conclusions. Hence, my agreement remains firm with option C being the best match.

Therefore, my answer is still Option C - X linked recessive. **(500 characters including spaces)**
